concerns.title: RedisSessionHandler::$baseData grows unbounded with distinct session ids for the worker's entire lifetime
concerns.location: src/Session/Handler/RedisSessionHandler.php:125 (declaration), :141 (write in read()), :115 (close no-op), :286 (destroy)
concerns.whatBreaks: On a long-lived OpenSwoole worker using the Redis session backend, every distinct session id that ever calls read() inserts a permanent entry into the per-worker $baseData map. It is never removed: close() (line 115) is a bare `return true;`, destroy() (line 286) only DELs from Redis, and there is no gc() cleanup (line 297 returns 0). The handler is a per-worker singleton (instantiated once in CoSessionManager::resolveHandler() at line 79), so the map persists across all requests the worker handles. baseData holds the FULL serialized session blob (up to ~16 KB each) captured at read time.
concerns.evidence: read() at line 141 does `$this->baseData[(string)$sessionId] = $base;` unconditionally on every session read; $base is the raw serialized session string. No unset path exists anywhere in the class. Memory math: with churning visitors (each new visitor = new PHPSESSID), at average 2 KB serialized session, 1M distinct sessions over the worker's life ≈ 2 GB of PHP heap PER WORKER on top of the array bucket overhead (~100+ bytes/entry zend_string + HashTable bucket ≈ another ~150 MB). A 16-worker box serving a public site at ~50 new sessions/sec hits ~1M distinct sessions/worker in roughly 5.5 hours of even distribution; workers OOM or must be force-recycled within hours-to-a-day. This is the exact 'fresh process per request is gone' hazard.
concerns.mitigation: Unset the snapshot at session close: in close() iterate/clear, and more precisely clear the entry when the session lifecycle ends. The correct fix is to clear $baseData per-request — baseData is only needed between this request's read() and write(); after write() (or in close()) `unset($this->baseData[$sid])` for the session(s) touched this request. Also unset in destroy(). Since the snapshot is per-request transient (only used for the 3-way merge inside the same write()), it should never outlive the request — clearing it in close() bounds the map to in-flight concurrency (≤ pool/coroutine count) instead of total-sessions-ever.

concerns.title: No DB connection pool — 'one connection per coroutine' under HOOK_ALL coroutine concurrency exhausts MySQL/Postgres max_connections at trivial node counts
concerns.location: CLAUDE.md DB note ('one connection per coroutine'); src/Learn/DB.php:23-47 (only PDO helper shipped); absence of any DbConnectionPool in src/Store/
concerns.whatBreaks: Under the documented coroutine DB pattern (global $wpdb = new wpdb() / new PDO() per request, isolated per coroutine), peak live DB connections = peak concurrent in-flight DB coroutines. OpenSwoole's max_coroutine defaults to ~100,000 PER WORKER and the framework does NOT lower it (app.php only sets max_coroutine if ZEALPHP_MAX_COROUTINE env is present). With the default ~4–16 HTTP workers, a burst of concurrent requests each opening its own connection blows past MySQL's default max_connections=151 (Postgres=100) almost immediately: just 16 workers each holding ~10 concurrent DB-bound coroutines = 160 simultaneous connect attempts > 151. New connections get 'Too many connections' (MySQL 1040 / PG 53300) and requests 500. There is a Redis pool but no equivalent DB pool — the framework explicitly downgrades the per-coroutine pool from 'correctness requirement' to 'optimization' and ships none.
concerns.evidence: CLAUDE.md: 'The rule is one connection per coroutine ... A per-coroutine connection pool (mirroring RedisConnectionPool) is now an optimization ... no longer a correctness requirement.' No src/ class implements DB pooling (grep: only RedisConnectionPool exists). src/Learn/DB.php caches ONE \PDO per path in a process-wide `static array $cache` (line 7,26,45) — which is the opposite anti-pattern: in plain coroutine mode that single handle is SHARED across all coroutines (the framework's own rule says sharing one handle interleaves wire frames and corrupts the socket), and in coroutine-legacy the class-static reset (ext-zealphp 0.3.25) wipes the cache per request → connect-per-request. Either way there is no bounded pool. Connection math: live_db_conns = Σ_workers(concurrent_db_coroutines); max_coroutine≈100k/worker provides no backpressure, so the ceiling is the DB server's max_connections (151/100), reached at ~150 concurrent DB ops cluster-wide.
concerns.mitigation: Ship a coroutine-aware DbConnectionPool mirroring RedisConnectionPool (Coroutine\Channel of N PDO/mysqli handles per worker, acquire/with/release), sized so pool_size × workers × nodes ≤ db_max_connections − headroom. Set a sane default max_coroutine (e.g. 3000) in app.php and document the formula. For the SQLite DB.php example keep one handle but gate it behind a Channel(1) per coroutine. Until then, pin DB-backed deploys to a small worker count and put a ConcurrencyLimitMiddleware (already exists) in front of DB routes to cap in-flight requests below max_connections.

concerns.title: Store::publish fan-out is O(workers × nodes) per message — single Redis instance saturates as the cluster grows
concerns.location: src/Store.php:570 (publish) + src/App.php:4760-4776 (wirePubSubBoot: per-worker subscriber) + src/Store.php:560-561 (receiver-count docblock)
concerns.whatBreaks: Every Store::publish is a single Redis PUBLISH, but Redis must copy the payload to EVERY subscribed connection, and the framework opens one SUBSCRIBE connection per worker per node (wirePubSubBoot registers an onWorkerStart hook → each worker spawns its own RedisPubSub runner). So a cluster of N nodes × W workers has W×N persistent subscriber connections on ONE Redis instance, and each publish does W×N payload deliveries through that single instance's single-threaded core. At 10 nodes × 32 workers = 320 deliveries/publish; at 100 nodes × 32 = 3,200 deliveries/publish. A modest 5k app-level publishes/sec at 100 nodes = 16M Redis-side message deliveries/sec on one core — the Redis instance's output buffers and CPU saturate (single-threaded PUBLISH dispatch is the documented Redis bottleneck) long before the app does. Pub/sub also can't be sharded across a Redis Cluster transparently (cluster PUBLISH is broadcast to all shards via the bus, making it worse, not better).
concerns.evidence: src/App.php:4760 `self::onWorkerStart(function(){ ... $pubsub->start(); })` runs per worker; RedisPubSub::runner (src/Store/RedisPubSub.php:146) opens its own `new RedisClient($this->url)` and SUBSCRIBEs. Store.php:560-561 explicitly states receiver count = `(subscribed workers per instance) × (instances)`. PUBLISH delivery cost in Redis is O(subscribers) on a single thread.
concerns.mitigation: (1) Run a dedicated per-node aggregator: one SUBSCRIBE connection per NODE (not per worker) that re-fans-out to local workers over an in-process channel / OpenSwoole Table signal — collapses Redis subscriber count from W×N to N and per-publish deliveries from W×N to N. (2) Offer a sharded pub/sub backend (NATS / Redis Cluster keyspace-sharded channels / Kafka) for clusters beyond ~16 nodes. (3) Document a hard guidance ceiling (e.g. 'pub/sub fabric is single-Redis-bound; expect to shard or move to a broker past ~500 total workers').

concerns.title: Session writeLock is ONE global Atomic for ALL sessions AND holds blocking disk I/O inside the critical section — serializes every session write across all workers and coroutines
concerns.location: src/Session/Handler/TableSessionHandler.php:56,186,194-235 (writeLock acquire at :198, writeFile under lock at :231); default handler resolved in src/Session/CoSessionManager.php:75-77
concerns.scaleAxis: concurrency / request-rate (concurrent session-bearing requests across all workers)
concerns.whatBreaks: TableSessionHandler is the DEFAULT session handler in coroutine mode (CoSessionManager::resolveHandler() maps null/'table' to TableSessionHandler::register()). It uses a SINGLE process-wide Atomic ($writeLock, static, one instance for the whole server) as a global mutex around EVERY session write — not per-session. Worse, the critical section (write(), lines 201-235) holds the lock across writeFile() (line 231), which does fopen('c') + flock(LOCK_EX) + ftruncate + fwrite + fflush + fclose — a blocking disk syscall. So ALL session writes server-wide are fully serialized behind one lock that is held for the duration of a synchronous disk write. Throughput collapses to 1/(disk-write-latency): on spinning disk or a contended fsync-heavy FS (~5-10ms per fflush) the ceiling is ~100-200 session writes/sec for the ENTIRE server regardless of worker count; on SSD (~0.2-1ms) the ceiling is ~1000-5000/sec. Because every authenticated request writes the session twice per request (CoSessionManager::__invoke seeds $g->session['__start_time']=microtime + ['UNIQUE_REQUEST_ID']=uniqidReal() on EVERY request at lines 237-238, dirtying the session so session_write_close always calls write()), a 32-worker box that could otherwise serve tens of thousands of req/s is hard-capped at the single-lock-plus-fsync rate the moment requests carry a PHPSESSID. The lock provides ZERO parallelism: N workers and M coroutines all queue on one Atomic.
concerns.evidence: Code: write() acquires the single static Atomic via `while (!$writeLock->cmpset(0, 1)) { Coroutine::usleep(500); }` (TableSessionHandler.php:198-200), then inside the same try-block (before `finally { $writeLock->set(0); }` at :234) calls `$this->writeFile($sessionId, $data)` (:231). writeFile() (:404-420) is blocking: flock($fp, LOCK_EX) + ftruncate + fwrite + fflush. The lock is class-static (`private static ?Atomic $writeLock` :56), allocated ONCE in register() (:110), shared across all forked workers (Atomic is pre-fork shared memory). Complexity: serialization is O(N_concurrent_writers) queue depth × O(disk_write_latency) hold time = global throughput bound of 1/disk_latency writes/sec irrespective of cores. The merge3/CAS retry loop (max 3 attempts, :194) ALSO runs inside the lock, so a conflict adds a full decode→merge3→encode pass under the global mutex. Every request dirties the session (CoSessionManager.php:237-238 unconditional writes), guaranteeing a write() on session_write_close for every session-bearing request.
concerns.mitigation: Two independent fixes, both needed: (1) Make the lock per-session-id, not per-server. Keep the optimistic-CAS path on the Table version column (it is already a lock-free CAS) and DROP the global Atomic entirely — OpenSwoole\Table::set is itself atomic per-row, so the version-CAS loop is sufficient for the in-memory tier; no global mutex is required to serialize independent session ids. (2) Move writeFile() OUT of the critical section — write-through to disk should happen AFTER the Table CAS commits and the lock (if any retained) is released, or be made async via a task worker / coroutine-deferred flush so the hot request path never blocks on fsync under a shared lock. Additionally, stop unconditionally dirtying the session every request (gate the __start_time/UNIQUE_REQUEST_ID writes behind 'session was actually mutated by the app') so read-only authenticated requests skip write() entirely. For multi-host or high-write deployments, document Redis (RedisSessionHandler, WATCH/MULTI per-key) as the scale path — its locking is per-key, not per-server.

concerns.title: Room pub/sub PSUBSCRIBE wakes EVERY worker on EVERY node per message — global fan-out, not targeted routing
concerns.location: src/WSRouter.php:439-445 (init PSUBSCRIBE 'ws:room:*'); src/WSRouter.php:775-835 (handleRoomMessage); src/Store/RedisBackend.php:535-538 (publish)
concerns.whatBreaks: Every room message is delivered by Redis to the single 'ws:room:*' PSUBSCRIBE held by EVERY worker on EVERY node, then each woken worker JSON-decodes the payload, checks str_starts_with, json_decode again, and only then discovers it holds zero local members and returns. A room with 5 members on node A still wakes all W×N workers cluster-wide. At 100k connections across, say, 10 nodes × 32 workers = 320 subscribers, every single room broadcast is amplified 320× in Redis deliveries + PHP decode work, regardless of how few clients actually need it. A modest 5k msgs/sec of room traffic becomes 1.6M Redis pub/sub deliveries/sec + 1.6M JSON decodes/sec of pure waste. Redis pub/sub is single-threaded on the publish dispatch path; this saturates the Redis CPU and every worker's event loop long before connection count itself is the limit.
concerns.evidence: init() registers exactly ONE pattern subscriber: App::subscribe(self::ROOM_CHANNEL_PREFIX . '*', ...) at WSRouter.php:439. RedisPubSub.runner() PSUBSCRIBEs that pattern per worker (wirePubSubBoot builds one RedisPubSub per worker in onWorkerStart, App.php:4760-4776). Redis PSUBSCRIBE 'ws:room:*' matches every ws:room:{name} channel, so a single Store::publish (one PUBLISH, RedisBackend.php:537) is delivered to all S=W×N subscribers. handleRoomMessage (817-818) computes $localMembers and returns early ONLY AFTER the wake+decode — the work to discover 'I have no members' is paid by every worker on every message. Cost per room message = O(W×N) deliveries + decodes, independent of actual recipients.
concerns.mitigation: Replace the single cluster-wide 'ws:room:*' pattern with targeted routing: maintain a server-set per room (which server_ids hold ≥1 member, derivable from ws_room_members.server_id) and PUBLISH only to ws:room:{name}:{serverId} channels for servers that actually hold members — each server SUBSCRIBEs only its own ws:room:*:{ownServerId} pattern. This makes per-message cost O(servers-with-members) instead of O(all-workers-cluster-wide). Alternatively move to one PSUBSCRIBE per ACTIVE room scoped to nodes holding members, or use Redis sharded pub/sub (SPUBLISH/SSUBSCRIBE, Redis 7+) to avoid the single-dispatcher bottleneck.

concerns.title: O(N) linear preg_match scan over all pattern routes per request — the dominant routing cost
concerns.location: src/ResponseMiddleware.php:669-674 (matchAndDispatch)
concerns.whatBreaks: Every request that is NOT an exact-string path match falls through a linear loop running one preg_match per registered pattern route until one matches. Exact routes (no regex metachars) get an O(1) hash hit at line 665, but ALL `{param}` routes, ALL nsRoute/nsPathRoute routes, and the framework's own implicit catch-alls (`/{file}`, `/{dir}/{uri}`, `/api/...`) are pattern routes scanned linearly. Because the implicit file/dir catch-alls are registered LAST and match almost anything, a request for a static-style or unmatched URL executes preg_match against EVERY user pattern route before reaching them. At 50 routes this is ~negligible; at 500 pattern routes it is ~500 preg_match/req; at 2000 (large WordPress/multi-tenant app) it is ~2000 regex evaluations per request on the worker's single coroutine-blocking CPU section. preg_match is not free (~0.3-1µs each compiled), so 2000 routes ≈ 0.6-2ms of pure CPU added to every request's critical section, capping per-worker throughput.
concerns.evidence: foreach ($app->routesByMethod()[$matchMethod] ?? [] as $route) { if (preg_match($route['pattern'], $uri, $matches)) { ... } } — a flat array per method, scanned front-to-back. No prefix tree, no static-segment bucketing, no first-segment index. routesByExactMethod() (line 665) only covers paths where isExactRoutePath() returns true (no regex metacharacters), i.e. literal paths only.
concerns.mitigation: Add a first-path-segment index: bucket pattern routes by their literal leading segment (e.g. all `/api/*` patterns under key 'api') computed at compileRouteTable(), then at match time scan only the bucket for the request's first segment plus a small 'wildcard' bucket. This turns O(total routes) into O(routes sharing the first segment) — typically a 10-50x reduction. For fully-literal-prefix routers a radix/trie on path segments eliminates the scan entirely. Cheap to add since the table is already rebuilt once at boot in compileRouteTable().

concerns.title: Class-static snapshot iterates the ENTIRE class table on every yield (ungated), making per-yield CPU O(total classes loaded)
concerns.location: ext-zealphp/zealphp.c:504 zealphp_statics_snapshot_save, called unconditionally from zealphp_on_yield (zealphp.c:1218) + zealphp_on_resume (zealphp.c:1280)
concerns.whatBreaks: Per-yield work grows linearly with the number of user classes resident in the worker, NOT with how many have statics. For a require_once-legacy app like WordPress/Drupal (1500-3000+ classes once warmed/preloaded), every coroutine yield walks all of them. Under HOOK_ALL a single WP request yields dozens-to-hundreds of times (every DB query, file read, outbound HTTP call yields), so the framework pays O(classes) x O(yields) per request. At ~2000 classes and ~100 yields/request that is ~200k class-table probes per request purely in scheduler-callback C code, on top of OpenSwoole's own context switch. CPU-bound throughput collapses well before the I/O concurrency the coroutine model promises.
concerns.evidence: zealphp_statics_snapshot_save does `ZEND_HASH_FOREACH_STR_KEY_VAL(EG(class_table), ...)` (line 522) visiting EVERY ZEND_USER_CLASS, only short-circuiting AFTER the lookup via `if (ce->default_static_members_count == 0) continue;` (line 526). It is NOT guarded by any *_active flag in on_yield (lines 1218-1221) — unlike fn-statics (gated by zealphp_fn_statics_active) and globals (gated by zealphp_coro_globals_hooks_active). So it runs every yield in coroutine-legacy regardless of whether any class actually uses statics. on_resume calls the mirror restore, doubling it.
concerns.mitigation: Mirror the fn-static design: maintain a registry of only classes whose CE_STATIC_MEMBERS() is non-NULL (populate lazily when statics are first allocated, prune on class teardown like the fn_static_registry at zealphp.c:2099), and iterate that registry in zealphp_statics_snapshot_save instead of the full EG(class_table). Add a `zealphp_class_statics_active` gate so the walk is skipped entirely when no user class has statics yet. This turns per-yield cost from O(all classes) into O(static-using classes), the same decoupling already applied to function statics (comment at zealphp.c:657).

concerns.title: Every yield deep-copies all 7 superglobal arrays; per-yield cost scales with superglobal payload size
concerns.location: ext-zealphp/zealphp.c:1104 zealphp_snapshot_save (save) + zealphp.c:1134 zealphp_snapshot_restore (restore), unconditional in on_yield/on_resume
concerns.whatBreaks: Each yield ZVAL_DUPs the full contents of $_GET/$_POST/$_COOKIE/$_SERVER/$_FILES/$_REQUEST/$_SESSION into a fresh per-coroutine snapshot array; each resume deep-copies them back. $_SERVER alone is ~25-40 entries; a large $_POST (file upload metadata, big form), a fat $_SESSION (WordPress stores user/options blobs), or a $_REQUEST that duplicates $_GET+$_POST multiply this. With ~100 yields/request a request with a 50-entry aggregate superglobal set performs ~100 x 2 x 50 = 10,000 zval deep-copies + matching frees per request, all in scheduler-callback context that blocks the whole worker (single-threaded). This is pure overhead that raw OpenSwoole does not pay.
concerns.evidence: zealphp_snapshot_save loops `for (const char **n = sg_names; ...)` over all 7 names (sg_names at zealphp.c:79) and does `ZVAL_DUP(&copy, arr)` (line 1127) — a recursive deep copy of the whole array — for each present superglobal, every yield. zealphp_snapshot_restore (1134) does the symmetric ZVAL_DUP back into the live slot. Neither is gated; both run on every coroutine switch once wrappers are installed.
concerns.mitigation: Switch superglobals to the same COW-delta model already used for $GLOBALS (zealphp_coro_globals_deltas at zealphp.c:158): snapshot only keys whose value differs from a shared parent baseline, and rely on refcount-sharing (ZVAL_COPY) for unchanged arrays instead of ZVAL_DUP. $_SERVER is almost entirely request-invariant and need not be deep-copied at all. Alternatively, only snapshot superglobals that were actually written since the last resume (dirty-flag via a write hook), since most handlers read but never mutate them.

concerns.title: CoroutineMemorySessionHandler::$sessions accumulates per-(cid,sessionId) with a no-op close()
concerns.location: src/Session/Handler/CoroutineMemorySessionHandler.php:10 (declaration), :43 (write), :62 (close no-op), :68 (gc only on probabilistic trigger)
concerns.whatBreaks: The in-memory session handler stores `$sessions[$cid][$sessionId] = ['data'=>..., 'last_access'=>...]` (line 43). close() (line 62) is a no-op and destroy() (line 56) only removes a single session for the current cid. OpenSwoole recycles coroutine ids, so the outer map is bounded by the high-water cid value, but the INNER per-cid maps accumulate every distinct sessionId ever seen on that cid and are never pruned when the coroutine ends. Because cids are reused, a hot cid slot collects sessions from thousands of unrelated past requests, each holding the full serialized session payload.
concerns.evidence: write() at line 43 never evicts; close() returns true without touching $sessions; the only cleanup is gc() (line 68) which scans by last_access — but PHP's session gc runs only probabilistically (session.gc_probability/gc_divisor, default 1/100) and only if a session is actually started with gc enabled, which is unreliable on a daemon that bypasses PHP's request shutdown. With ~256 live cid slots and, say, 10k distinct sessions accumulating across each reused cid at 2 KB each, a worker holds ~256 × 10k × 2 KB ≈ 5 GB before any gc fires. Growth is O(distinct sessions per cid-slot) and effectively unbounded between gc passes.
concerns.mitigation: Clear the per-cid bucket in close(): `unset($this->sessions[Coroutine::getCid()][$sessionId])` after write/close (mirror what TableSessionHandler::close() does at line 139, which correctly does `unset($this->context[Coroutine::getCid()])`). Drive gc from a deterministic App::tick() timer on worker 0 rather than relying on PHP's probabilistic session gc, or simply make the handler request-scoped so each request's data is dropped on close.

concerns.title: WSRouter::$localRoomMembership leaks members on disconnect — release() does not prune room maps
concerns.location: src/WSRouter.php:154 (declaration), :571 release() (no room cleanup), :790-794 (only path that prunes, gated on a pub/sub 'leave' message)
concerns.whatBreaks: The per-worker `$localRoomMembership[$room][$clientId] = true` map is only ever pruned when a pub/sub 'leave' message arrives (line 792) AND the client is still in $localFds. The documented disconnect hook WSRouter::release() (line 571) unsets $localFds and the Store row but does NOT remove the client from $localRoomMembership. So a client that disconnects (onClose → release) without the app explicitly calling Room::leave() leaves a permanent {room → clientId} entry in the worker's heap. Over a long-lived worker with normal WebSocket churn (clients connecting/joining rooms then dropping), the map grows with cumulative distinct (room, client) pairs ever seen, never reclaimed.
concerns.evidence: release() body (lines 571-576) is only `unset(self::$localFds[$clientId]); Store::del(self::TABLE, $clientId); stats++` — no $localRoomMembership touch. The leave-prune at line 792 is reachable only via handleRoomMessage() on a 'leave' type, which requires the app to call Room::leave() before/independently of release(). A chat/presence app that calls only release() on disconnect (the documented onClose pattern at the file header line 27) leaks every room membership. At 100 conn/sec churn with ~3 rooms each, that's ~300 leaked entries/sec/worker × ~80 bytes ≈ 24 KB/sec/worker → ~2 GB/worker in ~24h on a busy node; also corrupts push fan-out (handleRoomMessage line 817 pushes to stale local members, guarded only by isEstablished()).
concerns.mitigation: In WSRouter::release($clientId), scan and unset the client from every room: `foreach (self::$localRoomMembership as $room => $members) { unset($members[$clientId]); if (empty) unset room; }` (O(rooms) per disconnect, cheap). Better: maintain a reverse index clientId→[rooms] so release() is O(rooms-for-that-client). Document that release() now also clears room membership so apps don't need a separate Room::leave() on hard disconnect.

concerns.title: Redis subscriber + pool connections scale as O(workers × nodes) with no cap — crosses Redis default maxclients=10000 at ~1000 worker-instances
concerns.location: src/App.php:4760-4789 (wirePubSubBoot, onWorkerStart per worker); src/Store/RedisConnectionPool.php:152-163 (ensureChannel pre-fills pool_size clients per worker); src/Store.php:122,142 (default pool_size=8)
concerns.whatBreaks: Steady-state Redis connections = pool_size×workers×nodes (data pool) + (1 pubsub + 1 streams runner)×workers×nodes (dedicated subscriber connections — each worker's onWorkerStart spawns its own RedisPubSub owning ONE dedicated connection, plus RedisStreams owning another). With the default pool_size=8: total ≈ 10 × workers × nodes. Redis/Valkey default maxclients=10000 is crossed when workers×nodes ≥ ~1000 — e.g. 32 workers × 32 nodes (10,240 conns), or 64 workers × 16 nodes. At that point new worker connects get '-ERR max number of clients reached', so a rolling deploy or autoscale event fails to bring up workers and the cluster silently loses pub/sub coverage on the unconnected workers.
concerns.evidence: RedisConnectionPool::ensureChannel() pushes `$this->size` (default 8) `new RedisClient` per worker on first acquire (lines 156-160). wirePubSubBoot registers an onWorkerStart that does `new RedisPubSub(...); $pubsub->start()` and `new RedisStreams(...); $streams->start()` — fired in EVERY worker (App.php:4760,4772,4776,4780,4786). RedisPubSub docblock: 'Owns ONE dedicated Redis connection (separate from the pool — SUBSCRIBE monopolises a connection)'. Computed: workers=32,nodes=20 → pool=5120 + subs=1280 = 6400; workers=64,nodes=20 → 10240+2560 = 12800 > 10000. Channels DON'T multiply the count (all channels multiplex onto the one SUBSCRIBE connection per worker — App.php:4773-4774), which is good, but the worker×node fan-out is unbounded.
concerns.mitigation: Document the connection budget formula (≈10×workers×nodes) and require raising Redis `maxclients` proportionally, or front Redis with a proxy (Twemproxy/Envoy/Redis Cluster) that multiplexes. Lower default pool_size for large fleets (pool_size is already configurable via conn['pool_size']). Consider a single shared subscriber connection per NODE (one process subscribing and fanning out to workers via OpenSwoole IPC) instead of per-worker, cutting subscriber connections from workers×nodes to nodes.

concerns.title: RedisStreams XACK and pub/sub dispatch open a fresh (non-pooled) Redis connection per message — connect/teardown churn unbounded by throughput
concerns.location: src/Store/RedisStreams.php:140-154 (dispatch: `new RedisClient($url)` per ACKed message); src/Store/RedisPubSub.php:120-129 (stop signaller new client)
concerns.whatBreaks: Every successfully-handled Stream message spawns a `go()` that constructs a BRAND-NEW RedisClient solely to XACK, then closes it (RedisStreams.php:147-149). This connection is NOT drawn from RedisConnectionPool — it's a full TCP+RESP handshake per message. At a sustained reliable-queue throughput of M messages/sec/worker, that's M new Redis connections/sec/worker of connect+auth+close churn on top of the steady pool. At, say, 2k msg/sec across a 16-worker node, that's 2000 short-lived connects/sec hammering Redis's accept loop and the local ephemeral-port/TIME_WAIT table; combined with the per-worker pools this can spike the live connection count well above the steady-state estimate during bursts and trip maxclients transiently, plus add ~0.2–1ms connect latency to every ack.
concerns.evidence: RedisStreams::dispatch (line 140) wraps each message in `go(function() { ... $ackClient = new RedisClient($url); try { $ackClient->xack(...); } finally { $ackClient->close(); } })`. Comment at line 141-143 acknowledges 'Each dispatch coroutine owns its own client — sharing the runner's read-client would race on the socket' — correct for safety but the fix was per-message NEW connection rather than acquiring from the pool. RedisClient::__construct → pickDriver → new PhpredisDriver/PredisDriver opens a non-persistent socket (connect(), not pconnect()). O(1 connection) per message → O(throughput) connection rate.
concerns.mitigation: Acquire the ack client from the worker's RedisConnectionPool via $pool->with(fn($c)=>$c->xack(...)) inside the dispatch coroutine instead of `new RedisClient`. The pool already serialises socket access safely per coroutine and caps total sockets at pool_size. Same fix for any other per-message `new RedisClient` site. If the stream runner needs its own pool, give RedisStreams a small dedicated pool rather than per-message construction.

concerns.title: WebSocket room broadcast delivered to every worker on every node even when zero local members hold the room
concerns.location: src/WSRouter.php:439-445 (single ws:room:* PSUBSCRIBE per worker) + src/WSRouter.php:775-835 (handleRoomMessage) + src/WS/Room.php:209-227,271-277 (publish)
concerns.whatBreaks: The 'one PSUBSCRIBE per worker covers every room' design (deliberately avoiding per-room subscriber proliferation) means EVERY room message — and every join/leave presence event — is delivered to all W×N workers, which each json_decode the payload, run the pattern-match, look up local membership, and only THEN skip if they hold no members. For a chat app with many small rooms spread across nodes, the vast majority of workers receive every message for rooms they have no members in. At 100 nodes × 32 workers, a single room with 5 members generates 3,200 wasted message deliveries + 3,200 json_decode/handler dispatches per push (only ~5 do real work). A 1,000-msg/sec room load becomes 3.2M decode+dispatch ops/sec cluster-wide, and the publishing path's Redis fan-out is the same W×N problem as concern #1. Presence churn (join/leave on every connect/disconnect) compounds it — each is its own full-cluster publish (Room.php:86,106).
concerns.evidence: WSRouter.php:439 `App::subscribe(self::ROOM_CHANNEL_PREFIX . '*', ...)` — a SINGLE pattern subscriber per worker matches ALL rooms. handleRoomMessage (WSRouter.php:817-818) does the local-membership filter AFTER decode: `$localMembers = self::$localRoomMembership[$roomName] ?? []; if ($localMembers === []) { return; }`. Room::join/leave (Room.php:86,106) each call `$this->publish(...)` → cluster-wide presence fan-out.
concerns.mitigation: Route room messages by OWNER LOCALITY instead of broadcasting: maintain a per-room set of server_ids that actually hold members (already have ws_room_members.server_id) and PUBLISH to per-owner-server channels (ws:server:{id}) like sendToClient does, OR subscribe each worker only to the specific ws:room:{name} channels it has local members for (dynamic SUBSCRIBE/UNSUBSCRIBE on join/leave) rather than the global `ws:room:*` pattern. This makes delivery O(servers-with-members) not O(all workers).

concerns.title: TieredBackend L1 invalidation publishes one message per write, fanned to every node — write-heavy tables defeat the cache and flood pub/sub
concerns.location: src/Store/TieredBackend.php:229-237 (set), 339-359 (incr/decr), 164-182 (publishInvalidation), 106-115 (enableInvalidation: one subscriber per node)
concerns.whatBreaks: Every write through TieredBackend (set/del/incr/decr) calls publishInvalidation → a Redis PUBLISH on `__l1_invalidate:{table}`, delivered to one subscriber per node (each TieredBackend's RedisPubSub). So cluster-wide invalidation traffic = write_rate × N node-subscribers, all through the single pub/sub Redis. For a hot counter (incr/decr evicts L1 on EVERY call — TieredBackend.php:344) the L1 tier provides near-zero benefit under contention while generating maximum invalidation traffic: a 50k incr/sec counter at 100 nodes = 5M invalidation deliveries/sec on the same single Redis that also serves the authoritative INCRs. The L1 cache becomes pure overhead (every write evicts it everywhere before the next read can hit) AND a pub/sub amplifier. The HMAC signing (computeHmac, TieredBackend.php:191) adds a hash_hmac per publish on top.
concerns.evidence: TieredBackend::set (line 233-235) `$this->publishInvalidation($name, $key)` on every write; incr (line 344) `$this->l1->del(...); $this->publishInvalidation(...)` evicts AND publishes per increment. publishInvalidation (line 177) `$this->l2->publish(...)`. enableInvalidation (line 109) `new RedisPubSub(...)` — one subscriber per TieredBackend instance per node.
concerns.mitigation: (1) Do not enable cross-node invalidation on write-hot tables — document that incr/decr-heavy tables should use plain RedisBackend (no L1) since L1 can't help under cross-node write contention anyway. (2) Batch/coalesce invalidations: debounce per-key publishes within a short window, or publish key-set deltas instead of one message per key. (3) Skip invalidation publish entirely for incr/decr (the L1 eviction is local; cross-node staleness is already bounded by l1Ttl) — only publish for set/del where a stale read is a correctness issue.

concerns.title: Subscriber-connection and runner-coroutine count scales as workers × nodes × distinct-channel-runners, multiplying Redis connection load
concerns.location: src/App.php:4760-4789 (one RedisPubSub + one RedisStreams runner per worker) + src/Store/RedisConnectionPool.php:46 (pool of 8 per worker, separate from subscriber conns) + src/Store/TieredBackend.php:109 (additional invalidation subscriber per node)
concerns.whatBreaks: Each worker holds: a RedisConnectionPool (default 8 clients), plus a dedicated RedisPubSub SUBSCRIBE connection if any App::subscribe handler exists, plus a dedicated RedisStreams connection if any subscribeReliable exists, plus per-dispatch ephemeral clients (RedisStreams::dispatch opens a NEW RedisClient per XACK — RedisStreams.php:147, and pub/sub dispatch handlers may too). Connection count ≈ N × W × (8 + 1 pubsub + 1 streams). At 100 nodes × 32 workers that is 100×32×10 = 32,000 persistent connections to one Redis instance — past Redis's default maxclients (10000) and into file-descriptor / memory-per-connection (~20KB output buffer each = ~640MB just for connection buffers) territory. The per-XACK fresh-client pattern (RedisStreams.php:147 `new RedisClient($url)` then close) adds connect/teardown churn proportional to message throughput on top.
concerns.evidence: App.php:4772 `new RedisPubSub(...)` + 4780 `new RedisStreams(...)` per worker in the onWorkerStart hook. RedisConnectionPool.php:15,46 default size 8 per worker. RedisStreams.php:147 `$ackClient = new RedisClient($url)` per acknowledged message. No global connection accounting or cap across workers.
concerns.mitigation: (1) Per-node connection aggregation (same fix as concern #1) collapses subscriber connections from W×N to N. (2) Reuse a small dedicated ACK client per RedisStreams runner instead of new-per-XACK (a size-2 ack pool avoids socket-race without connect churn). (3) Surface total-connection math in docs and recommend Redis maxclients tuning + per-node connection budgeting; consider a shared subscriber process (addProcess sidecar) per node instead of per-worker runners.

concerns.title: OpenSwoole\Table maxRows is a hard boot-time cap with NO eviction — set() silently returns false and the cache falls back to file-only, so the memory tier becomes useless under churn
concerns.location: src/Store/TableBackend.php:30-59 (make/set); src/Cache.php:124-128, 235-273 (init/set)
concerns.whatBreaks: Once a Table reaches maxRows (default Cache = 4096 rows), every new distinct key's set() throws OpenSwoole\Exception internally, is caught and returns false. There is NO LRU/LFU/FIFO eviction — the table just stays full of whatever keys happened to land first. For Cache, set() then writes file-only and spillsFull++ for every subsequent new key; get() for those keys always misses the ns memory tier and pays a file read (open+read+unserialize, ~10-100us + a disk seek). The 'in-memory cache' silently degrades to a slow file cache the moment the working set exceeds maxRows. Bites at exactly maxRows distinct hot keys (4096 default — trivially exceeded by any real cache workload; e.g. one row per user session on a 10k-user app).
concerns.evidence: new Table($maxRows) + $t->create() allocates a FIXED shared-memory segment at boot (TableBackend::make L32-43). set() catches OpenSwoole\Exception and returns false (L57-58) — no eviction path exists anywhere in the class. RAM is pre-allocated: row_size = ceil-sum of column byte sizes; Cache uses val=STRING(8192)+ttl=INT(4)+crc=INT(4) ≈ 8200 B/row, so 4096 rows ≈ 33 MB reserved whether full or empty. Raising maxRows to cover a 1M-key working set = 1M × 8200 B ≈ 8 GB RESERVED per worker-shared segment at boot. Also: OpenSwoole\Table is a fixed-slot open-hash — under hash collisions it can fail to insert BEFORE reaching the nominal maxRows (conflict-chain exhaustion), so effective capacity is ~0.8×maxRows.
concerns.mitigation: (1) Document that Table maxRows must be sized to the full hot working set up front (it cannot grow). (2) Add a real eviction policy to TableBackend (track an atime/insert-order column and evict-oldest on a full set() rather than silently failing) OR make Cache promote-on-file-hit + maintain an approximate-LRU so the bounded memory tier holds the *hot* subset, not the *first* subset. (3) For working sets > a few thousand keys, steer users to Store::BACKEND_REDIS with server-side maxmemory+allkeys-lru (already advised at Cache::init L116-122, but only when ttlSeconds is null — surface it for the Table backend too).

concerns.title: TableBackend::iteratePaged() is O(N²/page) — each page re-iterates from row 0 and skips $cursor rows
concerns.location: src/Store/TableBackend.php:134-161
concerns.whatBreaks: iteratePaged() treats the cursor as a decimal skip-offset and re-runs `foreach ($t as ...)` from the beginning every call, discarding the first $cursor rows. Paginating a full N-row table in pages of P costs page k = O(k·P) skipped iterations, so the total to drain is sum_{k} k·P = O(N²/P). A 100k-row Table paged at 100/page = ~1000 pages, last page skips 99,900 rows; total ≈ 5×10^9 row-visits to drain. The public Store::iteratePaged() docblock advertises this exact method 'for large tables where draining the full generator is impractical' — but on the Table backend it is MORE expensive than the single O(N) iterate(). Bites any UI/API that paginates a Table-backed table beyond ~10k rows (page latency grows linearly with page number; a 50k-row table's last pages take tens of ms of pure PHP foreach each).
concerns.evidence: L148-159: $skip=(int)$cursor; then `foreach ($t as $key=>$row){ if($i++<$skip) continue; ... }` — it iterates and skips $skip rows on EVERY call. There is no native SCAN cursor on OpenSwoole\Table, so resumption requires re-walking. Compare RedisBackend::iteratePaged (L301-336) which uses a real server-side SCAN/SSCAN cursor = O(page) per call.
concerns.mitigation: Document that iteratePaged() is O(N²) on the Table backend and not recommended above a few thousand rows — point large-table pagination at the Redis/Tiered backend (true cursor). If Table pagination must stay, cache the live iterator/position keyed by an opaque cursor token for the duration of a scan window (accepting the 'no consistency across cursors' contract already stated), turning resumption back into O(page).

concerns.title: RedisBackend::iterate() holds ONE pooled connection for N HGETALL round-trips — concurrent full-table scans starve the size-8 pool and throw acquire timeouts
concerns.location: src/Store/RedisBackend.php:253-280 (iterate); src/Store/RedisConnectionPool.php:63-78 (acquire timeout); src/Cache.php:417-427 (flush), 607-618 (gcMemory)
concerns.whatBreaks: iterate() does pool->acquire() once and holds that client for the WHOLE generator — SSCAN the membership set, then one HGETALL per key — releasing only in finally. For an N-key table that is N sequential round-trips on a single held connection: at ~0.2 ms LAN RTT, a 50k-key table iterate = ~10 s of wall time holding one of only 8 pool clients. Cache::gcMemory() (runs on a 60 s timer in worker 0) and Cache::flush() both drain the full iterate() generator. If a few requests trigger iterate()/flush()/gcMemory concurrently, 8 clients are tied up; the 9th concurrent Store op blocks in acquire() for the 5 s default timeout and then throws StoreException — surfacing as request errors unrelated to the slow scan. Bites whenever table size × RTT approaches the GC interval, or when >8 concurrent scans/large ops overlap.
concerns.evidence: L262/273 $client=$this->pool->acquire(); the loop yields per key fetching $client->hgetall(...) per row (L267/276); release only in finally (L270/279). Pool default size = 8 (Store.php L122). acquire() pops with 5.0 s timeout then throws (RedisConnectionPool L63-78). iterate() cost = 1 SSCAN-stream + N HGETALL = O(N) RTTs on one connection — there is no pipelined hydration in iterate() (only iteratePaged uses mhgetall, L326).
concerns.mitigation: Rewrite iterate() to hydrate in pipelined batches like iteratePaged (SSCAN a batch → one mhgetall → yield → release+re-acquire between batches so a long scan doesn't monopolize a pool client). Have Cache::gcMemory()/flush() use iteratePaged() (cursor batches) instead of draining iterate(). Consider a dedicated/separate connection for background scans so they never compete with request-path ops for the 8 request-path clients.

concerns.title: Spinlock busy-wait with fixed 0.5ms sleep, no backoff and no fairness — degrades super-linearly as concurrent writers to the global session lock pile up
concerns.location: src/Session/Handler/TableSessionHandler.php:198-200 (Coroutine::usleep(500) spin)
concerns.scaleAxis: concurrency (number of coroutines/workers simultaneously contending the single writeLock)
concerns.whatBreaks: The acquire loop is `while (!$writeLock->cmpset(0, 1)) { Coroutine::usleep(500); }` — a 0.5ms-quantum spin with no exponential backoff, no max-attempt cap, and no FIFO ordering. Combined with concern #1 (lock held across a multi-ms fsync), the wait is unbounded and unfair: when K coroutines contend, each loser re-attempts the CAS every 0.5ms, producing a thundering herd of cmpset retries on every lock release. There is no queue, so under sustained contention a given coroutine can be starved arbitrarily long (livelock-ish tail latency) while newer arrivals win the race. Tail latency for session writes balloons: at K=50 concurrent writers and a 5ms held lock, the 50th waiter sees ~250ms+ p99 just to acquire, on top of its own write. cmpset is a shared-memory atomic op (cache-line ping-pong across cores) — every spin iteration of every waiter bounces the lock's cache line, wasting memory bandwidth and burning scheduler turns that could run real work. There is also no upper bound on spin time, so if the lock holder stalls (slow disk, NFS hiccup) ALL session-bearing requests on the server hang together.
concerns.evidence: TableSessionHandler.php:198: `while (!$writeLock->cmpset(0, 1)) { Coroutine::usleep(500); }`. Fixed 500µs quantum, unconditional retry, no attempt counter, no break/timeout. Contrast with the bounded incrBounded loop in AtomicBackend.php:67 (capped at 100 attempts) — the session lock has NO cap. Because the held section includes blocking disk I/O (concern #1), the spin's expected wait = (queue_position × held_duration) where held_duration ≈ fsync latency (ms-scale), so wait time is O(K) per acquire and aggregate wasted-spin CPU is O(K^2) cmpset attempts per drain of the queue. No fairness guarantee → unbounded tail.
concerns.mitigation: If a lock is retained at all (concern #1 ideally removes it), replace the busy-spin with a fair, bounded primitive: an OpenSwoole\Coroutine\Channel of capacity 1 per session-id acts as a FIFO mutex (pop to acquire, push to release) — coroutines block in-scheduler with no CPU spin and FIFO fairness. Add a max-wait timeout so a stalled holder fails the request fast (503) instead of hanging every session. If keeping cmpset, add exponential backoff with jitter (0.5ms → cap at e.g. 8ms) and an attempt ceiling that converts to a timeout error. Best: eliminate the cross-session lock entirely (per-row Table CAS needs none) so this spin only ever contends among coroutines touching the SAME session id — a far smaller K.

concerns.title: Per-message room fan-out spawns one coroutine + getClientInfo syscall per local member — O(local_members) with per-push syscall overhead
concerns.location: src/WSRouter.php:822-834 (handleRoomMessage push loop); src/WSRouter.php:845-862 (pushWithBackpressure)
concerns.whatBreaks: For each room message a worker receives, it loops over every locally-owned member and does Coroutine::create(...) → pushWithBackpressure → isEstablished($fd) + getClientInfo($fd) + push($fd). getClientInfo is a non-trivial OpenSwoole call that reads connection metadata for the send_queue_bytes backpressure check. A 50k-member broadcast room sharded across 10 nodes = 5k local members per node; one broadcast spawns 5k coroutines and issues 5k getClientInfo + 5k push syscalls PER NODE PER MESSAGE. At 100 broadcasts/sec that is 500k coroutine spawns/sec/node and 1M syscalls/sec/node just for the backpressure probe + push. The coroutine-per-push (line 831) multiplies scheduler pressure: each push is cheap but the go() wrapper, the isEstablished+getClientInfo pair, and coroutine create/destroy churn dominate.
concerns.evidence: handleRoomMessage (822): foreach (array_keys($localMembers) as $cid) { ... Coroutine::create(fn => pushWithBackpressure(...)) }. pushWithBackpressure (851): $info = $server->getClientInfo($fd) on EVERY push to read send_queue_bytes (852). No batching — OpenSwoole has no multi-fd push, but the per-fd getClientInfo is an avoidable per-message cost. The go() per member (831) is O(local_members) coroutine allocations per message.
concerns.mitigation: Hoist the slow-consumer probe: getClientInfo is only needed when a queue is plausibly building — sample it every Nth push or cache send_queue_bytes per fd with a short TTL instead of querying on every message. Drop the per-member Coroutine::create for small rooms (push is already non-blocking in OpenSwoole); only fan out to coroutines in batches (e.g. one coroutine per 256 fds) so scheduler churn is O(local_members/256) not O(local_members). Consider a dirty-fd set so backpressure is checked lazily on the fds that actually have a non-empty send queue.

concerns.title: ws_owner / ws_room_members on Table backend are hard-capped at master fork and default to 4096 / 16384 — cannot reach 100k connections single-node, and Table backend is single-node-only
concerns.location: src/WSRouter.php:93-96 (defaults 4096/16384); src/WSRouter.php:403-416 (Store::make caps); src/WSRouter.php:542-565 (own() throws CapacityException on full)
concerns.whatBreaks: On the default Table backend the ownerCapacity (4096) and roomMembersCapacity (16384) are HARD caps allocated as fixed OpenSwoole\Table segments at master fork. Reaching 100k connections requires WSRouter::init(ownerCapacity: 100000, roomMembersCapacity: ...) or the cluster-wide guarantees silently don't exist — Table is single-process shared memory (cross-worker, NOT cross-node), so a multi-node 100k deployment MUST use Redis. If an operator forgets to bump caps, own() throws CapacityException at 4096 connections (line 564) and new clients are refused. Even sized correctly, Table memory is pre-allocated: ws_owner at 100k rows × (server_id 192B + conn_id 32B, ×4 internal + ~32B/row overhead) ≈ ~100MB pre-allocated per server regardless of actual usage; ws_room_members with a 1M-pair cap at (room 64 + client_id 128 + server_id 192 + int 8) ×4 ≈ ~1.6GB pre-allocated. The defaults are explicitly 'demo-grade'.
concerns.evidence: static int $ownerCapacity = 4096; static int $roomMembersCapacity = 16384; (lines 94-96). Store::make(self::TABLE, self::$ownerCapacity, [...]) (403). own() rolls back and throws CapacityException 'table full at N connections' when Store::set returns false (542-564). Comment at 358-359: 'Table HARD CAP'. CLAUDE.md confirms Table 'maxRows is a HARD CAP allocated at master fork: RAM ≈ maxRows × (4 × Σ column sizes + ~32 B/row)' and is 'NOT cross-machine'.
concerns.mitigation: Make Redis backend mandatory (fail-fast at init) for any multi-node WS deployment — the docs say so but init() silently accepts Table. Raise the default caps or compute them from an expected-peak hint, and emit a boot advisory when Table backend + WS rooms are combined (Table can't federate). Document the pre-allocation RAM formula in init() so operators size ownerCapacity against real peak, not the 4096 demo default.

concerns.title: Stale-server GC does full O(N_owners + N_room_pairs) Store::iterate scans on the Table/Redis tables every 60s, and reaps a dead node's rows one DEL at a time
concerns.location: src/WSRouter.php:917-950 (runStaleServerGC); src/WSRouter.php:953-971 (sweepThisServer); src/WSRouter.php:701-707 (onlineByServer); src/Store/RedisBackend.php:253-280 (iterate = SSCAN + HGETALL per row)
concerns.whatBreaks: When a node dies, GC (worker 0 of one server, every 60s) iterates the ENTIRE ws_owner table AND the ENTIRE ws_room_members table to find rows whose server_id is dead, then issues one Store::del per matched row. On Redis backend, Store::iterate is SSCAN of the membership set followed by an HGETALL round-trip PER ROW (RedisBackend.php:276). At 100k owners + (say) 500k room-member pairs, a single GC pass is ~600k HGETALL round-trips through an 8-deep connection pool, plus up to 600k individual DEL/SREM calls to reap one node's share. That is minutes of Redis traffic per GC cycle and blocks the pool for other ops. With node churn (autoscaling, deploys) GC runs constantly and never catches up; dead rows accumulate, inflating onlineCount() and every subsequent iterate. in_array($server_id, $dead) inside the loop is also O(dead) per row.
concerns.evidence: runStaleServerGC (930): foreach (Store::iterate(self::TABLE) ...) with in_array(...,$dead) then Store::del per match; (936) same full iterate over ROOM_TABLE. RedisBackend::iterate (275-278) does sscan then hgetall($this->rowKey(...)) for EVERY row — one round-trip per row. No pipelining of the DELs. sweepThisServer (957-969) has the same full-table-iterate shape on graceful stop. onlineByServer (701) also full-iterates ws_owner.
concerns.mitigation: Maintain a reverse index server_id → SET of its owned client_ids (and room keys) so reaping a dead node is SMEMBERS + pipelined UNLINK of exactly that node's rows — O(rows_owned_by_dead_node) with batched deletes, never a full-table scan. Pipeline the DEL/SREM. Convert in_array($dead) to a hash-set lookup. Stagger GC so it processes a bounded budget of rows per tick rather than the whole table in one pass.

concerns.title: OPTIONS and 405 responses do a full O(total-routes × all-methods) table scan with preg_match
concerns.location: src/ResponseMiddleware.php:616-624 (OPTIONS) and 682-692 (405 Allow-header build)
concerns.whatBreaks: To build the `Allow` header, both the OPTIONS handler and the 405 path iterate `routesByMethod()` across EVERY method bucket and run preg_match on every route in each, breaking only on first match per method. This is a full cartesian scan of the route table — strictly more work than the normal match. CORS-heavy SPAs/APIs send an OPTIONS preflight before most cross-origin mutating requests, so for a write-heavy API roughly half the request volume can hit this full-table-scan path. At 1000 routes spread over 5 methods that is up to ~5000 preg_match per preflight, on the worker critical section. A 405-spray (clients probing wrong methods) is a cheap asymmetric DoS: each bogus request forces a full-table scan.
concerns.evidence: foreach ($app->routesByMethod() as $m => $routes) { foreach ($routes as $route) { if (preg_match($route['pattern'], $uri)) { $allowed[] = $m; ... break; } } } — nested loop over all method buckets; same pattern duplicated at OPTIONS (616) and 405 (682). The OPTIONS path additionally runs even when App::when middleware exists because it's handled before the when-chain.
concerns.mitigation: Reuse the per-first-segment index proposed above so the Allow-header scan only touches candidate routes. Additionally, precompute a path→allowed-methods memo (bounded LRU like when_middleware_memo) keyed on the matched route pattern, since the set of methods for a given URI is static after boot. For OPTIONS, short-circuit `OPTIONS *` (already done) and cache the per-pattern method set on the route struct at compile time.

concerns.title: buildServerVars rebuilds the entire $_SERVER array (strtoupper/strtr over every header) on every request — twice per dispatch in coroutine-legacy
concerns.location: src/App.php:1788-1857 (buildServerVars), invoked at registerOnRequest:7719 and again via rebindRequestInput:1913 at every handler/include dispatch
concerns.whatBreaks: Per request, buildServerVars loops every entry of $request->server AND every request header doing strtoupper + strtr('-','_') string allocations, then merges several default arrays. That's O(headers) string ops + a full associative-array copy on each request. In coroutine-legacy mode this runs in registerOnRequest AND is recomputed from scratch inside rebindRequestInput before EVERY handler and EVERY App::include() dispatch (line 5254) — so a legacy app that includes multiple files per request rebuilds the whole $_SERVER N+1 times. Requests with many headers (typical browser ~20-40, plus proxy/CDN-injected headers can reach 50-100) multiply the cost. At high RPS this is steady allocator pressure (GC/zend_mm churn) in the request critical section.
concerns.evidence: foreach ($request->server as $sk => $sv) { $srv[strtoupper($sk)] = ...; } foreach ($request->header as $key=>$value){ $srv['HTTP_'.strtr(strtoupper($key),'-','_')] = $value; } — full reconstruction, no caching. rebindRequestInput() calls buildServerVars($req) every invocation (App.php:1913) and executeFile() calls rebindRequestInput() on every include (App.php:5254).
concerns.mitigation: Cache the built $_SERVER on the RequestContext after the first buildServerVars() call for the request and have rebindRequestInput() reuse it (only overlaying the per-include PHP_SELF/SCRIPT_NAME/SCRIPT_FILENAME keys), instead of rebuilding from the request each time. The header transform result is identical across the N includes of one request — compute once per request, not once per include.

concerns.title: Per-coroutine snapshot memory is a full state copy x concurrent-coroutine count — RSS multiplier at 1000s of in-flight coroutines
concerns.location: ext-zealphp/zealphp.c: zealphp_coro_snapshots (53), zealphp_coro_globals_deltas (158), zealphp_coro_static_snapshots (88), zealphp_coro_fn_static_snapshots (94), zealphp_coro_constant_snapshots (81), zealphp_coro_ini_snapshots (85)
concerns.whatBreaks: While a coroutine is suspended, it retains a deep-copied snapshot of: all present superglobals (zealphp_coro_snapshots), its $GLOBALS delta (deep-copied arrays, zealphp.c:1008), its touched class-static values, its fn-static values, and constants/ini. For a WordPress-class request that is realistically tens-to-hundreds of KB of duplicated PHP arrays per coroutine. At 1000-2000 concurrent in-flight requests per worker (the scale coroutine concurrency is sold on), that is hundreds of MB to multiple GB of snapshot heap ON TOP of each request's own working set — vs raw OpenSwoole which keeps only the live arrays. Workers OOM or hit the OpenSwoole memory limit far below the connection ceiling.
concerns.evidence: zealphp_globals_snapshot_save does `ZVAL_DUP(&copy, uv)` per changed global (line 1008, explicitly 'deep-copy: no COW alias shared across coroutines'); zealphp_snapshot_save ZVAL_DUPs each superglobal (1127); zealphp_statics_snapshot_save ZVAL_DUPs each scalar/array static (546). Each snapshot is stored under the coroutine pointer key and lives until on_resume/on_close. The number of live snapshot sets equals the number of currently-suspended coroutines, so total snapshot RAM = O(concurrent_coroutines x per_request_isolatable_state).
concerns.mitigation: (a) Land the superglobal COW-delta change above so the dominant snapshot (superglobals) shares storage with the parent instead of deep-copying. (b) Document and enforce a per-worker concurrency cap (max_concurrency / max coroutines per worker) sized from measured per-request snapshot bytes, so admission control rejects/queues rather than OOMing. (c) Surface snapshot heap in App::stats() (count of live entries in the per-coro tables x avg size) so operators can see the multiplier before it bites.

concerns.title: Globals isolation does two full EG(symbol_table) scans per save plus per-restore (reset-to-parent), O(user-global count) per yield
concerns.location: ext-zealphp/zealphp.c:988 zealphp_globals_snapshot_save -> zealphp_globals_reset_to_parent (927); also called from zealphp_globals_snapshot_restore (1054) and request_end (2245)
concerns.scaleAxis: data-size (number of user globals) x request-rate (yields/request)
concerns.whatBreaks: In coroutine-legacy with globals isolation active, each yield walks the whole symbol table once to build the delta (snapshot_save) and AGAIN to reset every non-superglobal slot to the parent baseline (reset_to_parent). Each resume walks it once more to reset, then applies the delta. Legacy global-scope-include apps (Stage 8 / WordPress wp-admin) hoist many bare top-level vars into $GLOBALS ($menu, $submenu, $wp_*, $table_prefix, dozens of WP globals), so G is large. Combined with the class-table and superglobal walks, the fixed per-yield overhead is 3 full state-structure traversals, multiplying the WP yield-storm cost.
concerns.evidence: zealphp_globals_snapshot_save: `ZEND_HASH_FOREACH_STR_KEY_VAL(&EG(symbol_table), ...)` pass 1 (998) + pass 2 tombstone scan over the parent (1021), then unconditionally calls zealphp_globals_reset_to_parent() (1040) which does its own `ZEND_HASH_FOREACH_STR_KEY_VAL(&EG(symbol_table), ...)` (942) plus a parent re-install scan (962). snapshot_restore calls reset_to_parent again (1054). So save = ~3 symbol-table passes, restore = ~2, per yield.
concerns.mitigation: Track a dirty-set of globals written since the last resume (hook the global-write path) so snapshot_save and reset_to_parent only touch changed/added keys instead of re-scanning the entire symbol table every yield. The reset-to-parent 'belt-and-suspenders' double call (yield + restore, noted at zealphp.c:1051) can be reduced to once when a preceding on_yield is guaranteed.

concerns.title: HOOK_ALL turns every blocking call into a yield, so the per-yield overhead is paid per-I/O-op, not per-request — the cost is request-pattern-dependent and worst for chatty legacy apps
concerns.location: src/App.php (hookAll/HOOK_ALL resolution ~line 7341 region) + ext-zealphp on_yield/on_resume (zealphp.c:1192/1238)
concerns.scaleAxis: request-rate x fan-out (I/O operations per request)
concerns.whatBreaks: The isolation overhead is amortized per YIELD, and coroutine-legacy runs with HOOK_ALL so every DB query, every file stat/read (require_once-heavy WordPress does hundreds of file ops), every Redis/memcache/HTTP call yields. A legacy app that issues 200+ I/O operations per request therefore triggers 200+ full save/restore cycles, each paying the class-table walk + superglobal deep-copy + globals scans above. The same framework on a lean modern app with 2-3 yields/request is fine — so the overhead is invisible in microbenchmarks and explodes precisely for the WordPress/Drupal workloads coroutine-legacy targets.
concerns.evidence: on_yield (zealphp.c:1192) and on_resume (1238) chain into OpenSwoole's PHPCoroutine::on_yield/on_resume, i.e. they fire on EVERY coroutine switch. Under Runtime::HOOK_ALL (the coroutine-legacy default per the lifecycle matrix in CLAUDE.md) every hooked stream/socket/file op switches the coroutine, so the snapshot cost is per-I/O-op. CoSessionManager request-end resets (zealphp_reset_request_rtcaches/statics/class_statics, zealphp.c-side, called src/Session/CoSessionManager.php:303-338) add a further O(op_arrays)+O(class_table) pass once per request.
concerns.mitigation: (a) Document the yields-per-request sensitivity so operators benchmark with realistic I/O fan-out, not hello-world. (b) Reduce yield count for require_once-heavy apps via opcache (already recommended) and class/file preloading so file-stat yields drop. (c) Offer a fast-path that skips the full save/restore when a coroutine yields and resumes without any other coroutine running in between (single-in-flight detection) — the snapshot is only needed when state could be observed by a peer.

concerns.title: Cache file tier grows monotonically on disk for never-expiring keys (maxFiles defaults to 0 = unlimited)
concerns.location: src/Cache.php:44 ($maxFiles default 0), :85/:100 (init default), :559 writeFile, :607 gcMemory/gcFiles, :646 (maxFiles trim only when >0)
concerns.whatBreaks: Cache::set() always writes a file-tier copy (writeFile, line 562) and the memory tier is a hard-capped OpenSwoole Table that SPILLS overflow to the file tier. The file-tier GC (gcFiles, scheduled via registerGc on worker 0) only unlinks EXPIRED files; entries written with ttl=0 (the default for Cache::set) never expire and are never evicted because the maxFiles cap defaults to 0 (unlimited, line 100/646). So an app using Cache as a general key-value store with distinct keys and no TTL grows the on-disk cache dir without bound — eventually exhausting inodes/disk and, on each gcMemory/gcFiles pass, an ever-larger glob() (line 627) of the directory.
concerns.evidence: writeFile (line 562) is called unconditionally in set(); gcFiles drops files only on `ttl>0 && ttl<time()`; the maxFiles eviction at line 646 is gated on `self::$maxFiles > 0`, which is false by default. The class docblock itself notes (line 33) 'Eviction: No LRU/LFU. Full memory tier spills to file-only.' For a worker spilling 1k oversize-or-overflow distinct keys/min with ttl=0, that's 1.4M files/day; glob() of that directory each GC tick becomes O(N) multi-second and the disk fills. Not a heap-OOM but a real unbounded-resource-over-worker-lifetime growth.
concerns.mitigation: Default Cache::init() to a sane non-zero $maxFiles (e.g. 100k) so the oldest-first trim at line 646 engages, OR refuse ttl=0 + oversize combos with a documented warning. Document that ttl=0 cache entries on the file tier are permanent and require an explicit maxFiles cap or periodic Cache::flush(). Replace the per-tick glob() with a bounded directory walk (readdir + early-stop) so GC cost doesn't scale with total file count.

concerns.title: Pool acquire blocks (5s) then throws under sustained concurrency > pool_size per worker — Store ops fail rather than degrade when DB-style fan-out saturates the 8-slot pool
concerns.location: src/Store/RedisConnectionPool.php:63-83 (acquire: ch->pop($timeout=5.0) → StoreException on timeout)
concerns.whatBreaks: The pool is sized pool_size=8 per worker and acquire() blocks up to 5s for a free client, then throws StoreException. Because every Store/Counter/publish op holds a pool slot for its FULL duration — including any HOOK_ALL yield on the Redis round-trip — a worker handling >8 concurrent coroutines that each touch the Store will queue the 9th+ behind the channel. If Redis is slow (GC, slow SCAN/iterate on a ttl-mode table which is O(N), or cross-region RTT), the 8 slots stay checked out, the queue backs up, and once a coroutine waits >5s it gets a hard StoreException → 500. iterate()/iteratePaged hold a slot for the entire generator lifetime (RedisBackend.php:262,273), amplifying starvation. This bites at modest concurrency (just 9+ simultaneous slow Store ops per worker), well below the connection-count limits.
concerns.evidence: RedisConnectionPool::acquire (line 74): `$c = $ch->pop($timeout)` with default $timeout=5.0; on non-RedisClient result throws StoreException 'acquire timed out after 5s (size=8)' (line 77-79). RedisBackend::iterate acquires a dedicated client held across the whole generator (line 262 `$client = $this->pool->acquire()` ... yield loop ... finally release line 270). ttl-mode count()/iterate() are O(N) SCAN (RedisBackend.php:232-240,261-272). With pool_size=8, the 9th concurrent Store op blocks; under a hot endpoint doing N>8 concurrent Store reads per worker the pool is the bottleneck, not Redis itself.
concerns.mitigation: Make pool_size scale with expected per-worker coroutine concurrency (document pool_size ≈ p95 concurrent Store ops per worker, default higher than 8 for Store-heavy apps). Add a fast-fail/grow option and expose pool_acquire_timeouts_total (already tracked in Stats) on /healthz so saturation is observable. For iterate(), document that holding a slot for a long generator starves the pool and recommend iteratePaged() (which releases between batches).

concerns.title: Subscriber receiver-count fan-out is O(workers × nodes) — every PUBLISH delivers to (and is processed by) workers×nodes subscribers, multiplying handler work and the connection load it generates
concerns.location: CLAUDE.md pub/sub note ('every WORKER across every NODE running a matching subscriber receives the message'); src/Store/RedisPubSub.php:187-204 (dispatch fans each message to go() per handler)
concerns.whatBreaks: Plain pub/sub (App::subscribe) delivers each PUBLISH to EVERY worker on EVERY node that subscribed — receivers = workers×nodes per message. The framework documents this as correct Redis semantics, but it means a single broadcast triggers workers×nodes independent handler executions, each in its own go() coroutine, and each handler that then touches Store/DB acquires a pool slot or opens a connection. On a 32-worker × 20-node cluster, one broadcast = 640 handler invocations cluster-wide; a 100 msg/sec broadcast channel = 64,000 handler executions/sec and a corresponding spike in pool acquisitions. This couples message-rate to total-connection-pressure multiplicatively, so what looks like low publish volume becomes large connection/CPU load as the cluster grows.
concerns.evidence: CLAUDE.md: 'every WORKER (across every NODE) running a matching subscriber receives the message. So 32 workers on one node + 32 workers on a peer node = receivers: 64 per PUBLISH.' RedisPubSub::dispatch (line 195-203) fires `go()` per handler per matching message, per worker. Receiver/handler count = (subscribed workers)×(nodes) = O(workers×nodes) per published message; downstream Store work in handlers multiplies pool/connection pressure by the same factor.
concerns.mitigation: For work-queue semantics (process each message ONCE per cluster) use the Streams consumer-group path (subscribeReliable) which round-robins across consumers instead of broadcasting — document this as the default for anything that does DB/Store work, reserving App::subscribe broadcast for genuinely fan-out-to-all cases (cache invalidation, presence). Cap per-handler concurrency and have handlers use the pool (not new connections) so fan-out can't translate into unbounded connection growth.

concerns.title: WSRouter stale-server GC does full-table iterate of ws_owner + ws_room_members every 60s — O(total cluster connections) SCAN+HGETALL on one worker
concerns.location: src/WSRouter.php:917-950 (runStaleServerGC) + src/WSRouter.php:490-493 (tick on worker 0) + src/WS/Room.php:129-178 (size/members iterate fallback)
concerns.whatBreaks: runStaleServerGC (worker 0 of one node, every 60s) iterates the ENTIRE ws_owner table AND the ENTIRE ws_room_members table to find rows belonging to dead servers (WSRouter.php:930,936). On the Redis backend Store::iterate is a full SCAN + per-row HGETALL (RedisBackend iterate). With 1M cluster connections and a 16K-default-but-bumped room-members table, every GC pass is ~1M+ SCAN/HGETALL round-trips concentrated in a single coroutine — at Redis ~100k ops/sec that is ~10+ seconds of one worker hammering Redis every minute, plus the `in_array($serverId, $dead, true)` per row is O(dead-servers) making it O(rows × dead-servers). When a node crashes and N rows go stale simultaneously, this pass blocks/serializes. Room::size()/members() also fall back to full-table iterate on the Table backend (Room.php:135-141,170-177), O(all members across all rooms) per call.
concerns.evidence: WSRouter.php:930 `foreach (Store::iterate(self::TABLE) as ...)` then 936 `foreach (Store::iterate(self::ROOM_TABLE) ...)` — both unbounded full scans, run unconditionally every SERVER_GC_INTERVAL_MS (60s). Line 931 `in_array((string)($row['server_id'] ?? ''), $dead, true)` is linear in dead-server count per row.
concerns.mitigation: (1) Maintain a reverse index server_id → [client_ids] (Redis SET per server) so GC reaps a dead server's rows via SMEMBERS + targeted DELs instead of scanning the whole keyspace — O(rows-of-dead-server) not O(all-rows). (2) Convert $dead to a hash-set lookup instead of in_array. (3) Use Redis key TTLs on owner/member rows refreshed by heartbeat so dead-server rows expire passively, eliminating the scan entirely for the common case.

concerns.title: Redis Streams runner does one XREADGROUP per registered stream sequentially per loop — head-of-line blocking and BLOCK-latency stacking as stream count grows
concerns.location: src/Store/RedisStreams.php:84-97 (per-consumer sequential XREADGROUP loop)
concerns.whatBreaks: The runner loops `foreach ($this->consumers as $entry)` doing one blocking XREADGROUP (BLOCK $blockMs, default 1000ms) per stream sequentially in a single coroutine (RedisStreams.php:87-92). With S registered streams, a stream at the end of the list waits up to (S-1)×blockMs before its turn if earlier streams keep timing out empty — worst-case wake latency for the last stream is ~S seconds at the default 1s block. The code comment even acknowledges 'Could be optimised by grouping same-group streams' but doesn't. As an app adds queues/topics (each subscribeReliable adds a consumer), latency for low-traffic streams degrades linearly. This is per-worker but the latency cliff is real for fan-in workloads with many streams.
concerns.evidence: RedisStreams.php:87 `foreach ($this->consumers as $entry)` → line 89 `$client->xreadGroup($entry['group'], ..., [$entry['stream']], $entry['batchSize'], $entry['blockMs'])` — one blocking call per stream, serial. Comment at line 85-86 admits the missing multi-stream grouping.
concerns.mitigation: Group consumers sharing (group, blockMs) into a single XREADGROUP with multiple STREAMS keys (Redis supports N streams in one XREADGROUP), so one blocking call services all same-group streams and the BLOCK latency is shared, not stacked. Or run one runner coroutine per stream so blocks are concurrent.

concerns.title: Cache file tier grows UNBOUNDED by default (maxFiles=0) and every GC tick stat-reads EVERY file; the maxFiles trim adds an O(F log F) usort with F filemtime() syscalls
concerns.location: src/Cache.php:80-100 (init default maxFiles=0), 559-563 (writeFile), 621-657 (gcFiles)
concerns.whatBreaks: Every Cache::set() writes a file (writeFile, L559-563) — one file per key in a single flat .cache/ directory, regardless of value size or memory-tier success. With the default maxFiles=0 and ttl=0 (no expiry), the directory grows monotonically forever: a write-heavy cache accumulates millions of *.cache files, exhausting inodes and slowing the directory's own metadata operations. Independently, gcFiles() runs every 60 s and: glob()'s ALL filenames into a PHP array (O(F) memory — ~100 B/path × F), then fopen+fgets+fclose EVERY surviving file to read its TTL line (O(F) syscalls/tick). At F=1M files that's 1M opens every 60 s just to GC, plus a ~100 MB glob() array. When maxFiles>0 it additionally usort()s the survivors with filemtime() (another F stat() calls) = O(F log F). Bites at hundreds of thousands of file-tier entries (oversize values + memory-tier overflow under churn both spill to file).
concerns.evidence: writeFile is called unconditionally on every set() (L260). filePath = single dir, no sharding (L554-556). gcFiles: glob(dir.'/*.cache') loads all paths (L627); foreach opens+reads first line of each (L630-640); maxFiles trim does usort by filemtime (L648-650) + per-file filemtime stat. Default maxFiles=0 means the trim branch never runs → unbounded (L100, L646).
concerns.mitigation: (1) Shard the file tier into subdirectories by hash prefix (e.g. .cache/ab/abcd….cache) so no single directory holds >a few thousand entries — fixes both inode pressure and dir-metadata slowdown. (2) Default maxFiles to a sane non-zero cap, or refuse ttl=0 + maxFiles=0 with a boot warning (the file tier is the one with no other bound). (3) Track file count/age in an OpenSwoole\Table or atomic so gcFiles doesn't have to glob+stat the whole directory each tick; only sweep when the tracked count exceeds the cap.

concerns.title: RedisBackend 'ttl'-mode count()/iterate()/clear() are O(N) full-keyspace SCAN MATCH — count() in particular walks the entire prefix every call
concerns.location: src/Store/RedisBackend.php:232-240 (count), 565-577 (countViaScan), 261-271 (iterate ttl), 354-364 (clear ttl)
concerns.whatBreaks: In ttl-mode (the recommended cache mode on Redis — Cache::init flips to mode=ttl when ttlSeconds is set, L113-114) there is no membership set, so count() falls to countViaScan(): a full `SCAN MATCH {prefix}:{table}:*` walking the ENTIRE keyspace and counting matches in PHP. That is O(keyspace) per count() call — not O(table). On a shared Redis with millions of keys, a single Cache::stats()/Store::count() triggers a full-keyspace SCAN that returns every batch over the wire to PHP just to increment a counter. SCAN is non-blocking server-side but still touches every key and pays many RTTs (batch 200 → keyspace/200 round-trips). Bites when count() is called on a hot path (e.g. stats endpoint, healthz, a per-request size check) against a large/shared Redis.
concerns.evidence: count() routes ttl mode to countViaScan (L235-236); countViaScan scans prefix.'*' and counts in PHP (L569-576). scanKeys batch default 200 (PhpredisDriver L335). MATCH is applied client-... server-side filtering but SCAN still iterates the whole keyspace cursor (O(total keys), not O(matching keys)). iterate()/clear() ttl paths share the same full-SCAN shape (L264, L356).
concerns.mitigation: Avoid count() on ttl tables in hot paths — document it as O(keyspace). If an approximate size is acceptable, maintain a best-effort counter via INCR/DECR on set/del (drifts on TTL expiry, but Cache tolerates approximate memory_entries). Or isolate cache keys in their own Redis logical DB so SCAN MATCH walks only the cache keyspace, not the whole shared instance.

concerns.title: Cache::getOrCompute stampede lock allocates an unbounded number of named Atomics and serializes hot-key recomputation through a coarse 20ms spin
concerns.location: src/Cache.php:192-223 (per-key Counter lock '__cache_lock_'.md5($key), 20ms usleep spin); src/Counter/AtomicBackend.php:21-23 (Atomic map grows per name)
concerns.scaleAxis: data-size (distinct cache keys) × concurrency (concurrent misses on the same hot key)
concerns.whatBreaks: Two coupled issues. (a) Atomic-map growth: each distinct cache key that ever hits getOrCompute() creates a new named Counter `'__cache_lock_' . md5($key)`, and on the default Atomic backend AtomicBackend::atomic() does `$this->counters[$name] ??= new Atomic(0)` — the per-worker map grows by one Atomic PER UNIQUE KEY and is NEVER reclaimed (no TTL, no eviction; AtomicBackend::expire() is a documented no-op returning false). A workload with millions of distinct keys (per-user, per-URL cache fragments) leaks Atomics unbounded in every worker — each Atomic is a shared-memory slot allocated at first touch; this is a slow memory blowup that ends in OOM. (b) The lock is only meaningful per-worker on the Atomic backend (the Atomic IS shared cross-worker, so cross-worker herd suppression does work via shared memory — but the loser path spins `usleep(20_000)` × 10 = up to 200ms then computes anyway), so under a genuine herd on one cold-but-expensive key the losers each burn up to 200ms of wall time before either serving the cached value or double-computing. Coarse 20ms quantum means latency is quantized: a compute that finishes in 25ms still makes losers wait a full 40ms (two ticks).
concerns.evidence: Cache.php:192-194: `$lockName = '__cache_lock_' . md5($key); $lock = new Counter(0, $lockName); if (!$lock->compareAndSet(0, 1))`. Counter(0, $name) with explicit name calls setIfAbsent (Counter.php:81-83) which on AtomicBackend creates a permanent Atomic (AtomicBackend.php:46-59) keyed by name in a map with no eviction. Loser loop Cache.php:195-206: `for ($i=0;$i<10;$i++){ usleep(20_000); ... }` = 200ms ceiling, then `return $compute()` (:211) — herd not fully suppressed, just thinned. No cap on the number of distinct lock names → unbounded Atomic allocation, O(distinct_keys) shared-memory slots per worker.
concerns.mitigation: Bound the lock namespace: hash the key into a fixed-size lock-stripe array (e.g. 1024 pre-allocated Counters indexed by crc32(key) % 1024) instead of one Atomic per key — caps memory at O(stripes), trades a small false-sharing rate for bounded allocation. Replace the 20ms fixed spin with a Channel-based wait or shorter jittered backoff so latency isn't quantized to 20ms steps. Document that getOrCompute on the Atomic backend with high key-cardinality leaks lock Atomics, and recommend the Redis/Tiered backend (where lock keys carry TTL via EXPIRE) for high-cardinality stampede protection.

concerns.title: CircuitBreaker shared Atomic state means one worker's transient failures trip the breaker for EVERY worker — a per-server kill-switch, not per-connection
concerns.location: src/Store/CircuitBreakerBackend.php:62-65 (Atomic state slots), 128-155 (recordFailure trips OPEN), 163-206 (all reads/writes gate on shared state)
concerns.scaleAxis: nodes/workers (the failure-count threshold is aggregated across all workers sharing the Atomic)
concerns.whatBreaks: All breaker state (state, failureCount, firstFailureAt, openedAt) lives in OpenSwoole\Atomic slots that are shared across every worker process. The failure threshold (default 5 within a 10s window) is therefore a SERVER-WIDE aggregate, not per-worker or per-connection: failures observed by ANY worker increment the same shared failureCount. On a 32-worker box, 5 unrelated transient StoreExceptions spread across 32 workers (each seeing <1 failure) collectively trip the breaker OPEN, after which ALL 32 workers fast-fail every read (fallback) and throw on every write for openDurationSec (30s). This is correct circuit-breaker semantics, but it means the trip sensitivity does not scale with worker count — the SAME absolute threshold (5) that is reasonable for 1 worker is hair-trigger for 32 workers (each worker tolerates only 5/32 ≈ 0.16 failures before the shared counter trips).
