Conversely the misclassification caveat (every StoreException counts, including schema errors — documented at :48-54) means a single buggy code path emitting StoreExceptions from one worker can knock the entire fleet's Store offline. Severity is medium because it is opt-in (apps must explicitly wrap RedisBackend) and the fail-fast is by design — but the non-scaling threshold is a genuine tuning trap at high worker counts.
concerns.evidence: CircuitBreakerBackend.php:78-81 allocates the four state slots as `new \OpenSwoole\Atomic(...)` — shared memory, visible across workers (confirmed by the class docblock :44-46: 'all state lives in OpenSwoole\Atomic slots so transitions are visible across coroutines AND across workers'). recordFailure() (:141) does `$count = $this->failureCount->add(1)` on the shared slot and trips OPEN at `$count >= $this->failureThreshold` (:150). Default failureThreshold=5 (:71). So trip sensitivity per worker = threshold / worker_count — does NOT scale; at 64 workers the breaker trips on ~0.08 failures/worker. callRead/callWrite (:163,191) and iterate (:281) all short-circuit on the shared state.
concerns.mitigation: Scale the failure threshold with worker count (e.g. default threshold = base × max(1, worker_num) or expose a per-worker-normalized threshold) so trip sensitivity is constant as the fleet grows. Implement the planned typed StoreUnavailableException (noted on the v0.2.42 roadmap at :52-54) so only genuine transport failures — not schema/validation errors — increment the shared counter, preventing one buggy call path from fast-failing the whole fleet. Document that failureThreshold is a SERVER-WIDE aggregate and must be raised proportionally to worker_num.

concerns.title: Per-message Store::publish acquires from an 8-deep per-worker Redis pool — publish throughput is capped at pool_size concurrent in-flight publishes
concerns.location: src/Store/RedisBackend.php:535-538 (publish via pool->with); src/Store/RedisConnectionPool.php:48 (default size 8); src/WSRouter.php:614 (sendToClient publish), 626 (broadcast publish)
concerns.whatBreaks: Every sendToClient and every room broadcast does Store::publish → pool->with(fn($c)=>$c->publish(...)), acquiring one of 8 pooled clients per worker. Under HOOK_ALL the publish yields on the socket, so the coroutine holds the client across the round-trip. With only 8 clients per worker, at most 8 publishes can be in-flight per worker at once; a burst of room broadcasts (e.g. a chat spike) queues coroutines waiting on the pool channel. Combined with the global fan-out concern (every worker also RECEIVES every room message), the same pool is contended by both the publish path and any handler that re-publishes. At high broadcast rates the pool acquire becomes the throughput ceiling and adds publish latency (pool_acquire_timeouts surface in Store::stats).
concerns.evidence: RedisBackend::publish (537): $this->pool->with(fn(RedisClient $c) => $c->publish(...)). RedisConnectionPool constructor default int $size = 8 (line 48). CLAUDE.md: 'RedisConnectionPool is a per-worker Coroutine\Channel of N (default 8) clients ... each op acquires a private client.' broadcast (WSRouter.php:626) and sendToClient (614) both route through this single pool.
concerns.mitigation: Use a dedicated, larger publish-only pool (or a separate fire-and-forget PUBLISH client that doesn't block on a reply) for the hot WS broadcast path so it doesn't contend with read ops; PUBLISH returns a count but apps rarely need it synchronously. Make pool_size configurable per-deployment and size it against peak concurrent publishes per worker. Track pool_acquire_timeouts_total (already in H5 stats) as the saturation signal.

concerns.title: Server-wide heartbeat/broadcast via getClientList runs O(connections/100) paginated syscalls on EVERY worker every tick
concerns.location: route/ws.php:223-243 (heartbeat tick, getClientList 100-page loop); docs/websocket.md:174-179,258
concerns.whatBreaks: The documented server-wide fan-out pattern (heartbeat, broadcast-to-all) pages getClientList in chunks of 100 because OpenSwoole caps find_count at 100. getClientList returns ALL connected fds on the server (not just this worker's), so EACH worker's heartbeat tick walks the entire connection set: O(total_connections / 100) getClientList syscalls, then isEstablished + push per fd. With W workers all running the same App::onWorkerStart tick (route/ws.php:223), the server enumerates its full fd list W times every 30s. At 10k connections/server × 32 workers, that is 32 × 100 = 3200 getClientList syscalls + 320k isEstablished/push attempts every heartbeat — and most pushes are redundant because each worker re-pushes to fds it may not own. Scales O(connections × workers) instead of O(connections).
concerns.evidence: route/ws.php:224 registers App::onWorkerStart(fn) so EVERY worker runs the tick; 229: $fds = $server->getClientList($startFd, 100) in a do/while paginating all fds; 230-238 isEstablished+push per fd. Comment (225): 'OpenSwoole caps getClientList find_count at 100 — paginate'. getClientList is server-scoped, so each of W workers re-enumerates the whole set.
concerns.mitigation: Run server-wide heartbeat/broadcast from a SINGLE worker (gate on $workerId === 0, the same pattern WSRouter already uses for GC at line 489) instead of every worker, eliminating the ×W amplification. Better: push from each worker only to fds it actually owns by iterating the per-worker $wsFdMap (App.php:7991) rather than the global getClientList, making heartbeat O(own_connections) with zero cross-worker redundancy.

concerns.title: Per-request security preamble runs parse_url ×2-4 + decode-until-stable + path normalization unconditionally on every request
concerns.location: src/ResponseMiddleware.php:452,467,478,480,523,532,596 (process())
concerns.whatBreaks: Before any routing, every request unconditionally runs: parse_url(PHP_URL_PATH), decodeUntilStable() (up to 10 rawurldecode passes, each allocating a new string), normalizeRequestPath() (explode + per-segment loop + implode), a preg_match traversal check, and 1-3 more parse_url(PHP_URL_QUERY) calls in the strip-trailing-slash / PATH_INFO branches. This is fixed per-request overhead independent of route count, but it is several string allocations + a regex on the hot path for 100% of traffic. For very long URIs (query-string-heavy API calls, long paths) the explode/implode and repeated decode scale with URI length. It won't fail at scale but it is constant tax on every request's latency floor.
concerns.evidence: $parsedPath = parse_url($uri, PHP_URL_PATH); ... $decoded = App::decodeUntilStable($rawPath); ... $path = App::normalizeRequestPath($rawPath); plus parse_url($uri, PHP_URL_QUERY) repeated in the strip_trailing_slash (596) and PATH_INFO (532) branches. decodeUntilStable loops up to maxIterations=10 calling rawurldecode each time.
concerns.mitigation: Parse the URI once into (path, query) at the top of process() and thread both down instead of re-calling parse_url in each branch. decodeUntilStable almost always stabilizes in 1 iteration — keep the cap but it's already cheap; the bigger win is collapsing the 2-4 parse_url calls to one. Consider gating normalizeRequestPath behind a fast `strpbrk($path, '/.')`-style pre-check so paths with no `//`, `/./`, or `..` skip the explode/implode entirely.

concerns.title: ob_start/ob_get_clean output-buffer push+pop on every route dispatch, even for JSON/array/int returns that never use the buffer
concerns.location: src/ResponseMiddleware.php:298 (dispatchMatched ob_start) and the ob_end_clean calls at 303,322,340,346,358
concerns.whatBreaks: dispatchMatched() unconditionally calls ob_start() before invoking the handler, then for the common API cases (Generator, int status, array/object→JSON, ResponseInterface, string return) immediately calls ob_end_clean() to discard the buffer it just created. The buffer is only actually consumed for the void+echo case (line 368). So pure-JSON API handlers — the dominant shape for an API server — pay an output-buffer allocate+free per request for nothing. It's a small constant (~hundreds of ns) but it's on 100% of dispatches and adds allocator/stack work under high RPS.
concerns.evidence: ob_start(); $object = call_user_func_array($handler,$invokeArgs); ... if (is_array($object)||is_object($object)) { ob_end_clean(); $body = json_encode($object); ... } — the buffer is created then discarded for every non-echo return shape. Only the final void branch (line 368) reads it via ob_get_clean().
concerns.mitigation: This is hard to avoid generically because the handler MAY echo, but for the raw-route fast path (dispatchRawRoute, which already does NOT ob_start) the model exists. Consider a per-route flag set at registration when the handler is known to return-only (no echo) — or accept this as negligible. Lowest-priority item; documented for completeness.

concerns.title: Per-request middleware onion + param-extraction allocations scale with per-route/when-middleware count and matched-param count
concerns.location: src/ResponseMiddleware.php:245-251 (dispatchWithMiddleware), 640-643 (when chain), 671 (array_filter param extraction)
concerns.whatBreaks: For routes carrying per-route or App::when middleware, a fresh MiddlewareFrame object is allocated per middleware per request (the chain is rebuilt every request — only the instances are reused, the wrapper frames are not). A route with K middleware allocates K MiddlewareFrame + 1 RouteDispatchHandler objects per request. Plus, on every pattern match, line 671 allocates a closure and runs array_filter over the regex $matches to strip numeric keys. None of this is O(routes), but it is per-request heap allocation that grows with middleware depth and is repeated for the when-chain independently. Deep middleware stacks (auth+ratelimit+cors+headers+logging = 5-8 frames) on a high-RPS endpoint produce steady short-lived object churn.
concerns.evidence: $handler = new RouteDispatchHandler(...); foreach (array_reverse($chain) as $mw) { $handler = new MiddlewareFrame($mw, $handler); } — new wrapper objects each request. Line 671: $params = array_filter($matches, fn($k)=>!is_numeric($k), ARRAY_FILTER_USE_KEY) allocates a closure + filtered array per matched pattern route.
concerns.mitigation: The MiddlewareFrame allocations are inherent to PSR-15 re-entrancy and are genuinely cheap; the array_filter+closure on line 671 can be replaced with a precomputed list of named capture-group keys per route (the route pattern's named groups are known at compile time) so param extraction is a direct keyed copy with no closure allocation or numeric-key scan. Low priority — only matters at very high RPS on param routes.

concerns.title: App::$pubsubRegistry / $reliableRegistry / WSRouter room-handler arrays append without dedup or removal under reload
concerns.location: src/App.php:77 $pubsubRegistry, :79 $reliableRegistry, :4608 subscribe() append; src/WSRouter.php:757/767 registerRoomMessageHandler/registerRoomPresenceHandler append
concerns.whatBreaks: App::subscribe() appends to $pubsubRegistry[$channel][] (line 4608) and the WSRouter room handler registrars append to $roomMessageHandlers[$room][] / $roomPresenceHandlers[$room][] (lines 757/767) with no dedup. These are normally boot-once, so steady-state is bounded. But with dev route hot-reload (App::reloadRoutes / devReload) re-including route files that call subscribe()/onMessage(), and per the docs boot-infra is supposed to detect App::$reloading and skip — IF an app registers room/pubsub handlers outside that guard (e.g. inside a route registrar that reload re-runs), each reload appends another copy. Over many reloads the handler lists grow and every published message fans out to N duplicated handlers (O(reloads) work per message + retained closures).
concerns.evidence: subscribe() at line 4608 is `self::$pubsubRegistry[$channelOrPattern][] = $handler;` with no presence check; unsubscribe() exists (line 4617) but is not called by reload. registerRoomMessageHandler (line 757) likewise appends unconditionally. Bounded in production (reload off), so low severity, but a dev worker left running with frequent saves accumulates handler closures and multiplies pub/sub dispatch cost linearly with reload count.
concerns.mitigation: Dedup on registration (skip if the same callable is already present for the channel/room) or clear the relevant registry entries at the start of reloadRoutes() before re-running registrars, mirroring how $when_middleware_compiled/$when_middleware_memo are reset (App.php:6748/6812). Document that pub/sub and room-handler registration must be guarded by App::$reloading like other boot infra.

concerns.title: Cache::set/mset and the Cache memory+file double-write make every cache write at least 2 I/O ops (Store write + file write), and Cache::mset is a per-key loop with no batching
concerns.location: src/Cache.php:235-273 (set double-write), 501-510 (mset loop), 454-493 (mget)
concerns.whatBreaks: Cache::set() always does BOTH a Store::set() AND a writeFile() (file_put_contents) on every call (L244/260) — so each cache write is a shared-memory write PLUS a synchronous filesystem write (and a serialize()). Under HOOK_ALL the file write is not coroutinized for local FS the way a socket is, so write-heavy cache traffic pays a real syscall per set on the request path. Cache::mset() (L501-510) just loops set() per item — N items = N Store writes + N file writes + N serializes, with none of the pipelined batching that Store::mset()/RedisBackend::mset() provide. A bulk cache warm of 10k items = 10k file_put_contents on the request/worker. Bites high-write cache workloads and bulk warms; the file write also makes Cache strictly slower than a plain Store for ephemeral data.
concerns.evidence: set() calls Store::set then unconditionally writeFile (L244, L260) — the file tier is written even when the value fits in memory. mset loops self::set (L504-507) — does NOT call Store::mset's pipelined path. Contrast Store::mset → RedisBackend::mset which pipelines into 1 RTT (RedisBackend L427-461).
concerns.mitigation: Make the file-tier write opt-in / async (only persist when the caller asks for durability, or defer file writes to a batched background flush) so the common ephemeral-cache path is memory-only. Route Cache::mset through Store::mset() for the memory tier (one pipelined write on Redis) and batch the file writes. Document that Cache writes are 2× the I/O of a raw Store write.
